patterns.description: CRITICAL: Frame each skill as a named PROBLEM → FIX. Not 'what it does' but 'what pain it eliminates'. mattpocock uses '#1: Agent Didnt Do What I Want → /grill-me'. This is what gets 116K stars.
'Straight from my .claude directory' — skills that feel PERSONAL (not corporate/generic) get massive adoption. Users trust 'this is what I actually use' over 'this is what you should use'.
Quote respected engineering books (Pragmatic Programmer, Kent Beck, Ousterhout). This signals authority and makes the skill feel grounded in proven wisdom, not AI-generated fluff.
Small, focused skills that COMPOSE > one mega-skill. Each solves ONE problem perfectly. Users pick what they need. This is why mattpocock has 12 skills not 1.
/setup-matt-pocock-skills asks user preferences (issue tracker, labels, doc location) ONCE, then all other skills consume that config. Genius: one-time setup enables everything.
CONTEXT.md = shared vocabulary between human and agent. Reduces verbosity 75%, improves variable naming, reduces tokens. THIS IS A NOVEL TECHNIQUE worth copying.
60K email subscribers drives discovery. Personal brand + skills = flywheel. Stars come from audience, not GitHub search.
Position skills as 'engineering fundamentals' not 'AI tricks'. TDD, architecture, debugging — timeless practices packaged for agents. This resonates with senior devs.
The install command is: npx skills@latest add owner/repo. NOT npx skills add. The @latest matters for CLI version.
claudemarketplaces.com/skills — 20,478 skills indexed. Sorted by installs. This is THE discovery platform. Skills must be indexed here to get visibility.
Top skill: find-skills with 1.8M installs. frontend-design: 493K. The marketplace SORTS by installs. Being listed there with high installs = compound growth.
AI & Agent Building category: 4,675 skills. Largest category. But also most competitive. Better to dominate a smaller niche (Skill Development: 120 skills only).
CodeRabbit, Airtable, SEO Robot, Vibe Prospecting, 1inch, AppSignal — PAID placements on marketplace. This means companies PAY for skill visibility. Opportunity for premium skills.
'Make money from your Skills. On Capafy, your Skill runs online 24/7 as an agent product, and you get paid every time someone uses it.' — Skills are monetizable on Capafy.
vercel-labs/skills and vercel-labs/agent-skills dominate top positions. Their npx skills CLI IS the install mechanism. Being in their ecosystem = visibility.
microsoft/azure-skills has 2.2M+ total installs across 6+ skills. Enterprise skills from credible orgs get massive installs via trust/branding.
Users invoke skills as /grill-me, /tdd, /diagnose, /zoom-out. The name IS the command. Names must be short, memorable, verb-first.
Map skills to development phases via slash commands.
Treat SKILL.md as trainable parameters of a frozen agent.
Collections with proper docs get stars.
Top repos have CONTRIBUTING.md with anatomy and quality standards.
Overview, When to Use, Quick Start, Process, Common Mistakes, Verification.
Before building: get the agent to GRILL you with questions. Forces alignment. Prevents the #1 failure (misalignment). This is mattpocock's most popular skill.
'Ultra-compressed communication. Cuts token usage 75% by dropping filler while keeping full technical accuracy.' — A skill that changes HOW the agent communicates, not WHAT it does.
'Compact the current conversation into a handoff document so another agent can continue.' — Skills for agent-to-agent workflows are valuable.
'Build a throwaway prototype to flesh out a design — either a terminal app or several radically different UI variations.' — Skills that change the APPROACH not just the output.
'Disciplined diagnosis: reproduce → minimise → hypothesise → instrument → fix → regression-test.' Structured debugging loop as a skill.
Most powerful technique: CONTEXT.md that defines project-specific jargon. Agent uses fewer tokens, names things consistently, navigates easier. 'The single coolest technique in this repo.'
'Tell the agent to zoom out and give broader context on unfamiliar code.' Simple but powerful: a skill that forces PERSPECTIVE shifts.
'Find deepening opportunities informed by domain language and ADRs. Run once every few days.' A MAINTENANCE skill — not one-shot, recurring value.

CRITICAL: Structure every prompt as Identity (who you are) → Instructions (what to do, rules) → Examples (few-shot) → Context (data). This order maximizes instruction following. OpenAI + Anthropic both confirm this hierarchy.
patterns.source: OpenAI prompt engineering guide + Anthropic building effective agents

patterns.description: LLMs respond better to REASONING than rigid rules. Instead of 'ALWAYS do X', explain WHY X matters. The model generalizes from understanding, not from constraints. Reduces brittleness, improves edge case handling.
Include 1-2 concrete input/output examples in skills. Models pattern-match against examples more reliably than abstract instructions. Show diverse scenarios, not just the happy path.
Use XML tags (<task>, <context>, <output>) to delineate logical sections in generated prompts. Models parse XML boundaries more reliably than markdown for structured input/output extraction.
patterns.source: OpenAI prompt engineering: Markdown + XML formatting

patterns.description: The most successful agent implementations use simple, composable patterns — NOT complex frameworks. Prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer. Build with LLM APIs directly.

Tool documentation for agents deserves as much effort as UI design for humans. Include example usage, edge cases, input format requirements, clear boundaries from other tools. Test how the model uses your tools and iterate.

Pattern: one LLM generates, another evaluates and provides feedback in a loop. Most effective when clear evaluation criteria exist and iterative refinement provides measurable value. Apply to skill quality improvement.

Reasoning models (o1, Claude thinking) work best with high-level goals, not step-by-step instructions. Give the goal, trust them to work out details. GPT models need explicit instructions. Know which you're targeting.

Never change multiple prompt sections at once. Change ONE thing, measure the effect, keep or revert. Build test fixtures BEFORE changing prompts. This is the SkillOpt principle applied to prompt iteration.
patterns.source: OpenAI: build tests before changing prompts + skill-creator methodology

patterns.description: For agentic tasks: instruct the model to plan extensively, reflect after each tool call, maintain a TODO list. Don't terminate early — decompose the query into all sub-requests and confirm each is completed.
patterns.source: OpenAI prompt engineering: agentic tasks section

patterns.description: CRITICAL for reputation: create skills that address pain points being discussed THIS WEEK on HN/Reddit/TechCrunch. Timing × relevance > raw quality. Search for 'developers frustrated with X 2026' to find live pain. The ai-debt-detector was created because 800+ HN upvotes were complaining about the exact problem.

Submit to EVERY marketplace simultaneously: awesome-cursor-skills, EricGrill/agents-skills-plugins, daymade/claude-code-skills, wshobson/agents, agentskill.sh/submit, AgentDepot.dev, skills.sh (via install telemetry). 13 GitHub topics. Release tag. Each adds a discovery surface.

Comment on viral threads with: (1) personal experience with the SAME problem, (2) what you built to solve it, (3) link. Lead with empathy not promotion. 'This resonates — I spent 3 days refactoring 20 min of agent code' > 'Check out my tool'. Data citations build credibility.

Claude Code plugin marketplace (.claude-plugin/marketplace.json) is now the PRIMARY install mechanism. Without it, you're invisible to Claude Code users. Format: name, owner, plugins array with source paths. Also need .codex-plugin and .copilot configs for multi-harness.

For discoverability: (1) 10-13 topics including 'cursor-skill', 'agent-skills', 'claude-code', 'ai-agent', (2) Description includes install command, (3) Release tag triggers GitHub's discovery feed. This is the minimum for being found via search.

DEV.to/blog titles must lead with the READER'S problem, not your solution. 'Your AI Agent Writes Code With Hidden Debt' > 'I Built ai-debt-detector'. The reader asks 'what's in it for me?' — answer that in the title.

philosophies.text: Skills for Real Engineers. Not vibe coding.
Software engineering fundamentals matter more than ever. Skills condense fundamentals into repeatable practices.
Developing real applications is hard. Approaches that OWN the process take away control. Skills should be small, easy to adapt, composable.
The rate of feedback is your speed limit. Never take on a task that's too big.
Invest in the design of the system every day.
The best modules are deep: a lot of functionality through a simple interface.
A ubiquitous language makes conversations among developers derived from the same domain model.
Skills encode the workflows that senior engineers use — packaged for AI agents
Treat the skill document as the trainable state of a frozen agent
A skill's quality is measured by the BEHAVIOR it produces, not the text it contains

techniques.name: Grill session before building
techniques.description: Get agent to ask detailed questions until every decision branch is resolved. Prevents misalignment (#1 failure mode).

techniques.name: CONTEXT.md shared language
techniques.description: Define project jargon in one doc. Agent speaks concisely, names consistently, reduces tokens 75%.

techniques.name: Red-green-refactor TDD loop
techniques.description: Write failing test → minimal fix → refactor. Applied to SKILLS: test with pressure scenarios → write skill → refine.

techniques.name: Diagnose loop
techniques.description: reproduce → minimise → hypothesise → instrument → fix → regression-test.

techniques.name: Architecture deepening
techniques.description: Run regularly to find 'deepening opportunities' — places where code can be made simpler via better abstractions.

techniques.name: Setup skill pattern
techniques.description: One-time setup skill asks user preferences, creates config file, all other skills consume that config.

techniques.name: Handoff document
techniques.description: Compact current conversation into structured doc for agent continuation.

techniques.name: Caveman compression
techniques.description: Communication mode that drops all filler, keeps technical accuracy. 75% token reduction.

techniques.name: Skill validation script
techniques.description: Automated checks: frontmatter, lines, sections, code, placeholders.

techniques.name: Identity-Instructions-Examples-Context
techniques.description: Structure every generated prompt in this order. Identity first (role/purpose), then rules, then few-shot examples, then dynamic context data. Proven by OpenAI and Anthropic to maximize instruction adherence.

techniques.name: Atomic prompt iteration
techniques.description: Change one thing at a time in a skill prompt. Measure before/after. Keep or revert. Never batch-edit prompts without measurement.

techniques.name: Composable agent patterns
techniques.description: 5 building blocks: prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer. Start simple, add complexity only when measured improvement.

techniques.name: Trending pain → viral skill
techniques.description: Search HN/Reddit/TechCrunch for THIS WEEK's developer complaints. Create a skill that solves the exact named problem. Submit to 5+ marketplaces same day. Comment authentically on the threads. Timing is everything.

techniques.name: Multi-marketplace shotgun
techniques.description: On publish: submit to awesome-cursor-skills, EricGrill, daymade, wshobson, agentskill.sh, AgentDepot, skills.sh. Set 13 GitHub topics. Create release tag. Update README badges. All in one pass.
